content.claim: In Hungary, community mobilization through the NGO 'Human Chain for Rural Schools' and village-level protests (Erdőkürt case) contested school closures in the absence of formal legal protections, while in Poland, village-level protests in 2000 resulted in mixed outcomes: two of four schools were saved and two closed
content.context: Fresh live research through the International comparison lens
limitations: Evidence is qualitative and drawn from specific villages; no systematic data on the frequency, success rate, or sustainability of community mobilization across Hungarian or Polish rural areas
Cases predate or occur independent of formal policy mechanisms (pre-2013 in Hungary; 2000 in Poland), limiting applicability to post-2013 centralized governance
No evidence that community mobilization can substitute for budget/decision-making authority when central government controls funding and employment
content.population: Population described in the cited finding
content.time_scope: As reported by the cited source
